messages.subject: [R-SIG-Mac]around old MacOS support
messages.body_plain:
My experience is that older Macs do not disappear. They end up in the hands of persons who cannot afford newer versions. We gave away old machines to Eastern Europe (at a time when they not yet tried to become members of the European Union) and to third world Africa. Some of it may end up with persons interested in statistics (not in our case but I am not in a statistics department). Before leaving the Carbon version I would like to see a detailled description of how to recompile R in MPW C. (If I understand Stefano, I guess to write such descriptions is not something he especially likes). This would show those inheriting (or installing) R on the older Macs how to compile a larger project which may be useful for them even if they will not work on R development any more. On the other hand some may take up  the task to keep Carbon-R alive for a while longer. This might give some feedback to Stefano (and he MIGHT like to give advice to those persons or continue working on it under those circumstances). As some of those will not have easy access to the Internet/Web, downloadable - offline transferable packages may help.

By the way: I used R a lot to produce and develop a large number of reasonably complex graphics (bar chart variety). For this carbonization of R was a big drop in performance. This seemed not to have been Stefanos fault but the use of the much slower carbonized version of the Waste (graphics) engine. I am only now in the process of switching slowly to OS X and did not start up R there at all. Use of more native graphics will probably help.

Peter Sint

At 11:56 Uhr +0100 14.11.2001, Stefano Iacus wrote:
>Thank you all for your comments. I'll take them into account.
>The real essential of what I meant was that: probably after 1.4 I'll not improve any further the Carbon Gui, I'll just recompile every release of R but very few bug fixing at each release. I probably will do that since the R kernel will not be substantially changed (release 2.x ? let's see.)
>
>Recompiling packages and R kernel is highly time consuming (trust me) and in same sense I enjoy it anyway, but it is a matter of fact that new iMacs are shipped since july with the OSX pre-installed. Old MacOS apple environments (like MPW for example) have been freezed as apple want developers and people to work on OS X.
>
>Probably I'm wrong, but people with old hardware that cannot objectively switch to OS X are probably limited in the choice of applications available just now. Professionals or university depts have to switch sooner or later (I guess sooner) to OS X otherwise they will be obliged to work with old releases of applications (and so why not with an old release of R ?).
>
>Stefano
-- 
Peter Paul Sint (sint@oeaw.ac.at, http://www.iwe.oeaw.ac.at/sint )
RU - Institutional Change and European Integration, 
Austrian Academy of Sciences ICE, Baeckerstr.13
A-1010 Wien/Vienna, Austria, EU 
vox +431-51581-445  (fax +431-51581-566)

messages.subject: [R-SIG-Mac]around old MacOS support
messages.body_plain:
Probably simple for you
Previously you wrote
src/macintosh/ReadMe.MPW in R-devel archive (Aug_22_2001 or later).
But where is this R-devel archive? (an ftp archive, or a CVS?)
I never found this

I searched
http://developer.r-project.org/
rsync on http://cran.r-project.org/
http://rsync.r-project.org/
http://cran.r-project.org/
ftp://cran.r-project.org/pub/R/src
ftp://ftp.stat.math.ethz.ch/Software/R
ftp://cran.r-project.org/pub/R/sources.html
(http://cran.it.r-project.org/	 does not exist)

in searches I end up most of the time with some site for the binaries
http://cran.r-project.org/bin/macos/
http://cran.r-project.org/bin/macosx/


I find also source of 1.3.1
http://cran.r-project.org/src/base/R-1.3.1.tgz

before I search longer I better ask.

Peter

>Peter,
>
>next version of R (and the currently R-devel.tgz archive) contains a detailed file and scripts that will allow anyone to recompile R with MPW C.
>
>I would be pleased if any of you wont to build R following the instructions in the src/macintosh folder.
>
>As for the graphic device: we are adopting a new engine for the graphics that is quite better (and fast on the carbon version too).
>But, for example, the porting to MacOS of the new APIs took me a lot of time for other things (such implementing new stuff or fixing bugs, writing docs)
>As R is getting more powerful and versatile the implementation for MacOS (non X) is harder and harder because most of developers are switching to OS X (Apple too btw) and abandon their tools.
>
>
>Stefano
>
>On Sabato, dicembre 1, 2001, at 12:15 , Peter Paul Sint wrote:
>>...
>>>Peter Sint
>>>
>>>Stefano

-- 
Peter Paul Sint (sint@oeaw.ac.at, http://www.iwe.oeaw.ac.at/sint )

A-1010 Wien/Vienna, Austria, EU

messages.subject: [R-SIG-Mac][Rd] Mac Test
messages.body_plain:
R 1.4.0 is now sinking into feature freeze, and I'd like some help
testing it under Macintosh (from Sys 8.6 to X).


I've put a copy of R140b1 at

http://www.ci.tuwien.ac.at/~iacus/R

there are two files: R140b1.sit and R140b1.sit.hqx

download only one of the two.

Many tests have been done but you help will be precious. Several things 
have been changed or implemented both in R internals and Mac GUI.

Problem reports to stefano.iacus@unimi.it or to r-sig-
mac@stat.math.ethz.ch, please.  (At this stage the features are frozen, 
so no feature requests ....)

thanks

Stefano

messages.subject: [R-SIG-Mac].RData on Mac OS X
messages.body_plain:
Hi all,

I recently updated my system from Mac OS 9.1 to OS X (Version 10.1).
How can I use my currently invisible .RData files (created with R 1.3.1) 
on the new system?
Is there a way to make these files visible/readable on Mac OS X?

Sorry for this OS X beginner question...
Any advice is welcome!

Christof

Hmm. I had no problem whatsoever reading my old OS 9 files, nor some
Windows

_Rdata files and Linux .Rdata files... What are you trying to do? I
usually

open files by 


<excerpt>load("path/to/my/files/.RData")

</excerpt>

It shouldn't be a problem that the file is hidden. You just have to
know

where it hides... :-)


But then, I'm running R from the command line under X11 and I'm used
to the

UNIX way of things. If all those file paths are irritating for you, you

could start up OS 9 and rename your files to something else (without a
dot

at the beginning), then load by


<excerpt>load(file.choose())

</excerpt>

which ought to appeal to you :-)



Hope that helps (if not, write back)


Kaspar Pflugshaupt


</excerpt>
--Apple-Mail-1--193580165--

You can rename it as you please, but naming it as .RData and putting it=20=

You can double click on the file and OS X should pop up a dialog asking=20=

you "which app to use" (say..),  just choose R.

I hope this helps you.

Stefano

messages.subject: [R-SIG-Mac].RData on Mac OS X
messages.body_plain:
In 10.1 in the Get Info dialog you can associate filename suffixes 
with applications.
This might work for double-clicking .Rdata. Of course, that only 
works if you can get the Finder to show it in the first place!

There probably are some shareware or freeware appliations that will 
cause files whose names begin with "." to be shown in the Finder. 
Browse http://www.versiontracker.com/macosx/index.shtml. I thought 
there was a System or Finder preference for this, but I couldn't find 
it.

-Don

messages.subject: [R-SIG-Mac]i get a lot of questions on this ...
messages.subject_clean: i get a lot of questions on this ...
messages.body_plain:
If you want to install R on Darwin you must do a number of other things. 
Not all
of it is necessary, but most of it will make your life easier (after a 
while), and it
will make your R faster. Of course you may decide to wait on R-1.4.0.

a. Install Fink from fink.sourceforge.net. Use Fink to install libz, 
libreadline,
atlas, libjpeg, libpng, f2c, dlcompat, tcl/tk, and maybe f77/g77. Make 
sure you source
the init.csh file that comes with Fink. This sets /sw/bin at the 
beginning
of your path and it sets DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH. You could also install all 
of Gnome
from Fink (if you want to use --gui=gnome in R).

b. Become an Apple developer (for free) and download and install the 
Apple
developer tools. Go to www.apple.com/developer or connect.apple.com

c. Download the latest version of R-1.3.1 from 
gifi.stat.ucla.edu/R/builds
and install in /usr/local

d. See if it works. If it complains about missing libraries, try

otool -L /usr/local/lib/R/bin/R.bin

and see what it wants. Provide the missing stuff from either Fink or
gifi.stat.ucla.edu/R/dylibs. In the same may you can use otool -L to see
what the tcl/tk or gnome modules are looking for, and the various
precompiled packages.

===
Jan de Leeuw; Professor and Chair, UCLA Department of Statistics;
US mail: 9432 Boelter Hall, Box 951554, Los Angeles, CA 90095-1554
phone (310)-825-9550;  fax (310)-206-5658;  email: deleeuw@stat.ucla.edu
homepage: http://www.stat.ucla.edu/~deleeuw
========================================================
           No matter where you go, there you are. --- Buckaroo Banzai
                    http://www.stat.ucla.edu/~deleeuw/sounds/nomatter.au
========================================================

messages.subject: [R-SIG-Mac]R 1.4.0 for MacOS(X)
messages.body_plain:
The latest (final) build of R 1.4.0 has been put in a temporary 
repository that soon will be moved to CRAN.

You can find it in the usual two forms (full or base+recommended)

full <- c( one archive only containing the base distribution and all the 
recommended packages )

The URL of the site is the following:

http://159.149.74.117/~web/140/


Have nice holidays and a happy new year.

				Stefano M. Iacus

messages.subject: [R-SIG-Mac]R-1.4.0 for MacOS X
messages.body_plain:
Get it from ftp://gifi.stat.ucla.edu in pub.

This contains the base files plus the recommended packages (except rpart,
which does not compile yet).

It is compiled under MacOS X 10.1.2 with the December 2001 version of
the Developer Tools, and with g77 from fink.sourceforge net. It has 
support
for gnome, X11R6, and tcl/tk, and it uses the newest ATLAS from fink. It
has two-level namespaces and has a libR.dylib included.

For R,bin you need on your system (remember to set DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH,
or --better-- install fink and compile these things yourself)

        /usr/lib/libSystem.B.dylib (compatibility version 1.0.0, current 
version 55.0.0)

messages.subject: [R-SIG-Mac]R 1.4.0 for Apple Machines (both Carbon and Darwin)
messages.subject_clean: R 1.4.0 for Apple Machines (both Carbon and Darwin)
messages.body_plain:
I have just uplodaed on CRAN both versions of R 1.4.0.

So starting from Dec 23rd, you'll find the builds for Carbon and Darwin 
directly on CRAN respectively at

http://cran.r-project.org/bin/macos	:	Carbon R (8.6 -> 9.2 and OSX)
http://cran.r-project.org/bin/macosx	:	Darwin/X11 R  (thanks to Jan!)


specific updates and bug fixing for the Carbon release can be found in

http://cran.r-project.org/bin/macos/base/CHANGES

along with the Carbon FAQ

http://cran.r-project.org/bin/macos/rmac-FAQ.html


Contributed packages for rm140 will come in 2002 !

stefano
messages.body_snippet:

directly on CRAN respectively at

http://cran.r-project.org/bin/macos	:

messages.subject: [R-SIG-Mac]another source of R
messages.body_plain:
You can also install the Darwin/X11 version of R-1.4.0 (base and
recommended) by using fink (see fink.sourceforge.net). This
version (maintained by Jeffrey Whitaker, jsw@cdc.noaa.gov)
uses f2c for the FORTRAN parts.

Fink is a package manager. If you have installed fink (and the
Apple Development Tools) then all you have to say once is

fink install r-base
fink install r-commended

and every once in a while

fink selfupdate
fink update-all

It also makes sense, once you are at it, to install dlcompat, atlas,
g77, and xdarwin from fink.
===

Hi:

I am a new comer to unix and am trying to install R on MacOSX. I have tried both "fink install r-base" and "fink install r-commended" but it does not work:

[gattuso:~] gattuso% fink install r-base
sudo /sw/bin/fink install r-base
Reading package info...
Information about 251 packages read.
Failed: no package found for specification 'r-base'!

Fink seems to work OK because I have successfully downloaded gmt (although I am  not able to run it yet...).

Any help would be appreciated.

Thanks!
jp

messages.subject: [R-SIG-Mac]R on OSX
messages.body_plain:
go to /sw/etc/fink.conf and change the Trees: line to

Trees: local/main stable/main stable/crypto unstable/main 
unstable/crypto local/bootstrap

then say "fink install r-base"

--- Jan

messages.subject: [R-SIG-Mac]Installing R on Mac OS X.1
messages.subject_clean: Installing R on Mac OS X.1
messages.body_plain:
Since December 22th, I'm trying to install R on my mac and it is not yet
running...
Where can I find a complete installation procedure (from A to Z) ?

I understand that before installing R, Xfree86 (for example) or Xdarwin has
to be installed first.

Then some libraries have to be added (but exactly which ones ?) using Fink
for more convenience. I read that "atlas" had to be installed but Fink can't
find it... Same for libz.
Also Fink finds that my installation of Xfree86 / Xdarwin is not complete
(some shared libraries are missing ?) although I used the 4.1.0 / 1.0.5
installer (Mac OS X package)...

Finally, R 1.4.0 has to be installed.
It seems (Jan de Leeuw said it on this mailing list on the 22th of December)
that R can be installed using Fink (fink install r-base / fink install
r-commended) but Fink doesn't fink thoses packages...

Probably I'm too used to Apple installations where any child could do it...

Best regards.

Marc Hannebert.

Mail : hannebert@mac.com

messages.subject: [R-SIG-Mac]Installing R on Mac OS X.1
messages.subject_clean: Installing R on Mac OS X.1
messages.body_plain:
On Wed, 26 Dec 2001, Marc Hannebert wrote:

> Since December 22th, I'm trying to install R on my mac and it is not yet
> running...
> Where can I find a complete installation procedure (from A to Z) ?
>
> I understand that before installing R, Xfree86 (for example) or Xdarwin has
> to be installed first.
<complicated stuff>
> Probably I'm too used to Apple installations where any child could do it...
>

Perhaps you should start off with the Carbon version, for which the
procedure is more Mac-like.  It is apparently a bit slower than the Darwin
version but I'm happy with it even on an iBook.

(a) Go to
  http://cran.r-project.org
and follow the link
    MacOS (System 8.6 to 9.1 and MacOS X)
and then
    base

(b) Download
    rm140_FULL.sit

(c) On my Mac this automatically triggers the UnStuff process, or you may
have to click on the file icon to unstuff it.

(d) Drag the rm140_FULL folder to a suitable place in the Finder

(z) Optionally, drag the <R> icon from that folder to the dock.


	-thomas
messages.body_snippet:
On Wed, 26 Dec 2001, Marc Hannebert wrote:

> Since December 22th, I'm trying to install R on my mac and it is not yet
> running...
> Where can I find a complete installation procedure (from A to Z) ?

messages.subject: [R-SIG-Mac]more on R installation
messages.body_plain:
Hi:

Jan recommended to modify fink.conf. I have tried to do it with pico 
but it says that it cannot write the file. I guess that it is a 
problem related to permissions. I then search the internet on how to 
change permissions which I tried unsucessfully:


[gattuso:/sw/etc] gattuso% ls -l
total 16
drwxr-xr-x  3 root  admin  264 Nov  4 22:52 alternatives
drwxr-xr-x  4 root  admin  264 Nov  4 22:52 apt
drwxr-xr-x  3 root  admin  264 Nov  4 22:52 dpkg
-rw-r--r--  1 root  admin    6 Nov  4 22:52 fink-release
-rw-r--r--  1 root  admin  387 Nov  4 22:54 fink.conf
drwxr-xr-x  3 root  admin   58 Nov  4 22:52 profile.d
[gattuso:/sw/etc] gattuso% chmod 777 fink.conf
chmod: fink.conf: Operation not permitted



What am I doing wrong?

Thanks,
jp

>  go to /sw/etc/fink.conf and change the Trees: line to
>
>  Trees: local/main stable/main stable/crypto unstable/main
>  unstable/crypto local/bootstrap
>
>  then say "fink install r-base"

-- 
____________________________________________________________________
Jean-Pierre Gattuso
Laboratoire d'OcÃ©anographie de Villefranche, UMR 7093 CNRS-UPMC
B. P. 28, F-06234 Villefranche-sur-mer Cedex - France
Voice: +33 (0)493763859 - Fax: +33 (0)493763834 - US fax: +1 (978) 477-8302
<mailto:gattuso@obs-vlfr.fr> - http://www.obs-vlfr.fr/~gattuso

messages.subject: [R-SIG-Mac]R-1.4.0 full
messages.body_plain:
In

ftp://gifi.stat.ucla.edu/pub/R-1.4.0-MacOSX-full.tar.gz

Compiled with g77, ATLAS, dlcompat.  Support for gnome, tcl/tk, MySQL,
RmSQL, RMySQL, RPgSQL, RPVM, netCDF. Binaries for more
than 150 packages.

This build includes rpart, which was not in the previous build (which
only included the recommended packages).

I will post complete installation instructions (including dylib
notes) asap. Maybe Stefano can put this one on CRAN.
===

Could you be a tiny bit more specific ? What were the commands and how
did they fail ?

--- Jan

On Monday, December 31, 2001, at 08:52 AM, PETER CHEN wrote:

>
> I ran examples "optim", library(nlme)

messages.subject: [R-SIG-Mac]R-1.4.0 MacOX/Darwin problem
messages.body_plain:
Peter

I do not have any of these problems, all your examples run fine. Could 
you
let ne know what Mac, and what Mac OS X you have ? One thing
I could imagine is that ATLAS uses Altivec instructions for single
operations, which means that these problems may occur CPU's
without Altivec. Maybe others could verify. Also, you do need OS X
version 10.1 at least, I think.

--- Jan

On Monday, December 31, 2001, at 09:38 AM, PETER CHEN wrote:

> Welcome to Darwin!
> [localhost:~] pc% R
>
> R : Copyright 2001, The R Development Core Team
> Version 1.4.0  (2001-12-19)
>
> R is free software and comes with ABSOLUTELY NO WARRANTY.
> You are welcome to redistribute it under certain conditions.
> Type `license()' or `licence()' for distribution details.
>
> R is a collaborative project with many contributors.
> Type `contributors()' for more information.
>
> Type `demo()' for some demos, `help()' for on-line help, or
> `help.start()' for a HTML browser interface to help.
> Type `q()' to quit R.
>
>> example(optim)
>
> optim> fr <- function(x) {
>    x1 <- x[1]
>    x2 <- x[2]
>    100 * (x2 - x1 * x1)^2 + (1 - x1)^2
> }
>
> optim> grr <- function(x) {
>    x1 <- x[1]
>    x2 <- x[2]
>    c(-400 * x1 * (x2 - x1 * x1) - 2 * (1 - x1), 200 * (x2 -
>        x1 * x1))
> }
>
> optim> optim(c(-1.2, 1), fr)
> $par
> [1] 1.000260 1.000506
>
> $value
> [1] 8.825241e-08
>
> $counts
> function gradient
>     195       NA
>
> $convergence
> [1] 0
>
> $message
> NULL
>
>
> optim> optim(c(-1.2, 1), fr, grr, method = "BFGS")
> $par
> [1] 1 1
>
> $value
> [1] 9.594955e-18
>
> $counts
> function gradient
>     110       43
>
> $convergence
> [1] 0
>
> $message
> NULL
>
>
> optim> optim(c(-1.2, 1), fr, NULL, method = "BFGS", hessian = TRUE)
> $par
> [1] 0.9998044 0.9996084
>
> $value
> [1] 3.827383e-08
>
> $counts
> function gradient
>     120       38
>
> $convergence
> [1] 0
>
> $message
> NULL
>
> $hessian
>          [,1]      [,2]
> [1,]  801.6881 -399.9218
> [2,] -399.9218  200.0000
>
>
> optim> optim(c(-1.2, 1), fr, grr, method = "CG")
> $par
> [1] 1 1
>
> $value
> [1] 4.509718e-17
>
> $counts
> function gradient
>     156       41
>
> $convergence
> [1] 0
>
> $message
> NULL
>
>
> optim> optim(c(-1.2, 1), fr, grr, method = "CG", control = list(type = 
> 2))
> $par
> [1] 1 1
>
> $value
> [1] 7.111235e-18
>
> $counts
> function gradient
>     139       33
>
> $convergence
> [1] 0
>
> $message
> NULL
>
>
> optim> optim(c(-1.2, 1), fr, grr, method = "L-BFGS-B")
> Illegal instruction
> [localhost:~] pc%
>
> This just one of examples.
>
> Peter
>
>
>
>> From: Jan de Leeuw <deleeuw@stat.ucla.edu>
>> To: "PETER CHEN" <mcpchen@hotmail.com>
>> CC: R-SIG-Mac@stat.math.ethz.ch
